questions.text: A 58-year-old patient presents to the emergency department with crushing substernal chest pain radiating to the left arm and jaw. The ECG shows ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. This finding is most consistent with which type of myocardial infarction?
questions.title: Acute Coronary Syndrome
questions.rationale: ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, and aVF indicates an inferior wall myocardial infarction. These leads view the inferior portion of the heart which is primarily supplied by the right coronary artery. This finding is consistent with a STEMI (ST-elevation myocardial infarction) which represents a complete occlusion of the coronary artery with resultant myocardial tissue death.

questions.text: A patient with acute coronary syndrome is administered fibrinolytic therapy. Which laboratory value is most important to monitor closely during the first 24 hours following this treatment?
questions.title: Fibrinolytic Therapy
questions.rationale: After fibrinolytic therapy, bleeding is the major complication. Hemoglobin and hematocrit are critical to monitor for acute blood loss. A falling hemoglobin/hematocrit can indicate bleeding which may be occult or internal. While cardiac markers, coagulation studies, and electrolytes are also important, detecting hemorrhage is the highest priority.

questions.text: The nurse is caring for a patient with unstable angina. Which statement best differentiates unstable angina from stable angina?
questions.title: Angina Assessment
questions.rationale: Unstable angina is characterized by symptoms that are increasing in frequency, duration, and severity, often occurring at rest or with minimal exertion. It represents a progression of coronary artery disease. Stable angina typically occurs with predictable levels of exertion and is relieved with rest or nitroglycerin within 5-10 minutes. Unstable angina is considered a medical emergency and often precedes a myocardial infarction.

choices.text: Unstable angina only occurs with heavy exertion
Unstable angina is always accompanied by ST elevation
Unstable angina is angina that occurs at rest or with minimal exertion
Unstable angina always resolves within 5 minutes of rest

questions.text: A patient presents with a heart rate of 150 beats/minute, wide QRS complexes, and hemodynamic instability with a blood pressure of 80/40 mmHg. The most appropriate immediate intervention is:
questions.title: Life-Threatening Arrhythmias
questions.rationale: Synchronized cardioversion is the treatment of choice for a patient who is experiencing a tachyarrhythmia with hemodynamic instability. The wide QRS suggests ventricular tachycardia, which with hemodynamic compromise requires immediate electrical cardioversion. Defibrillation is used for pulseless ventricular tachycardia or ventricular fibrillation. Medication administration or oxygen therapy alone would be insufficient given the patient's unstable condition.

choices.text: Administration of adenosine 6 mg IV push

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a patient with heart failure who is receiving furosemide (Lasix) 40 mg IV twice daily. Which electrolyte should be monitored most closely during this therapy?
questions.title: Heart Failure Management
questions.rationale: Furosemide (Lasix) is a loop diuretic that increases excretion of sodium, chloride, and potassium. Hypokalemia is a common side effect of loop diuretics and can lead to cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients with heart failure who may already be at risk. Monitoring potassium levels and replacing potassium as needed is essential to prevent these complications.

questions.text: A patient with a new transcutaneous pacemaker is showing regular QRS complexes after each pacemaker spike. The nurse recognizes this finding as:
questions.title: Cardiac Pacemakers
questions.rationale: Capture refers to the successful depolarization of the ventricles following a pacemaker stimulus, indicated by a QRS complex after each pacemaker spike. Capture indicates that the pacemaker is functioning properly and delivering sufficient energy to stimulate ventricular contraction. Failure to capture would show pacemaker spikes without following QRS complexes.

questions.text: A patient with infective endocarditis develops a new murmur. This finding most likely indicates:
questions.title: Valvular Heart Disease
questions.rationale: The development of a new murmur in a patient with infective endocarditis strongly suggests valve damage or destruction. Endocarditis involves infection of the heart valves, and as vegetations grow, they can destroy valvular tissue leading to regurgitation (insufficiency). This is a serious complication that may necessitate valve replacement.

choices.text: Valve perforation or destruction causing regurgitation

questions.text: A patient has been diagnosed with sepsis. Which of the following criteria is part of the definition of systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS)?
questions.title: Sepsis and SIRS
questions.rationale: SIRS criteria include: temperature >38°C or <36°C, heart rate >90 beats/minute, respiratory rate >20 breaths/minute or PaCO2 <32 mmHg, and white blood cell count >12,000/mm³ or <4,000/mm³ or >10% bands. A temperature above 38°C (fever) is one of these criteria, indicating a systemic inflammatory response.

questions.text: A patient presents with hypotension, tachycardia, decreased level of consciousness, and decreased urine output following a traumatic injury. The nurse recognizes these signs as consistent with which stage of shock?
questions.title: Shock Stages
questions.rationale: Progressive (compensatory) shock involves the body's compensatory mechanisms beginning to fail. Signs include hypotension, tachycardia, altered mental status, and decreased urine output. Initial (compensatory) shock would show normal blood pressure with tachycardia, while refractory shock would show profound hypotension unresponsive to interventions. Irreversible shock is characterized by cellular and organ death with multiple organ dysfunction.

questions.text: A patient with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) has the following arterial blood gas values: pH 7.30, PaCO2 52 mmHg, PaO2 58 mmHg, HCO3 24 mEq/L. These values indicate:
questions.title: ARDS Assessment
questions.rationale: The ABG values show respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35 with elevated PaCO2 >45 mmHg) combined with hypoxemia (PaO2 <80 mmHg). This is characteristic of ARDS, where impaired gas exchange leads to CO2 retention and inadequate oxygenation. The normal HCO3 indicates this is an acute process without metabolic compensation yet.

questions.text: A 45-year-old patient sustained burns to the anterior chest, entire right arm, and anterior left arm following a house fire. Using the Rule of Nines, what percentage of total body surface area (TBSA) is burned?
questions.title: Burn Assessment
questions.rationale: Using the Rule of Nines: anterior chest = 9%, entire right arm = 9%, anterior left arm = 4.5% (half of 9%). Total TBSA burned = 9% + 9% + 4.5% = 22.5%. The Rule of Nines assigns 9% to each arm, 18% to each leg, 9% to the head and neck, 18% to the anterior trunk, 18% to the posterior trunk, and 1% to the perineum.

questions.text: Using the Parkland formula, how much fluid should be administered in the first 8 hours for a 70 kg patient with 30% TBSA burns?
questions.title: Burn Fluid Resuscitation
questions.rationale: The Parkland formula is 4 mL × kg body weight × % TBSA burn, with half given in first 8 hours. For this patient: 4 mL × 70 kg × 30% = 8,400 mL total in 24 hours. Half (4,200 mL) should be given in the first 8 hours post-burn.

questions.text: A patient presents to the emergency department with decreased breath sounds, hyperresonance to percussion, and tracheal deviation away from the affected side following blunt chest trauma. These findings are most consistent with:
questions.title: Chest Trauma Assessment
questions.rationale: Tension pneumothorax presents with decreased or absent breath sounds on the affected side, hyperresonance to percussion, and tracheal deviation away from the affected side. It's a life-threatening emergency where air enters the pleural space but cannot exit, causing increasing pressure that compresses the lung and potentially shifts the mediastinum, compromising cardiac output.

questions.text: A patient with a spinal cord injury at C5 suddenly develops severe headache, bradycardia, hypertension, and flushing above the level of injury. The nurse recognizes these symptoms as:
questions.title: Spinal Cord Injury Complications
questions.rationale: These symptoms are classic for autonomic dysreflexia, a life-threatening condition that can occur in patients with spinal cord injuries at T6 or above. It's caused by an exaggerated autonomic response to stimuli below the level of injury, such as a distended bladder or bowel. The body's inability to regulate autonomic responses results in vasoconstriction below the injury and subsequent bradycardia and hypertension.

questions.text: During the triage of multiple casualties following a building collapse, which patient should receive the highest priority for treatment?
questions.title: Mass Casualty Triage
questions.rationale: In mass casualty triage, patients with compromised airway, breathing, or circulation who can be stabilized with immediate interventions receive highest priority. A patient with airway obstruction and respiratory distress needs immediate intervention to prevent death and has the highest chance of survival with prompt treatment. Patients with cardiac arrest in a mass casualty situation typically receive lower priority due to poor outcomes and resource requirements.

choices.text: A patient with multiple fractures but stable vital signs
A patient with partial airway obstruction and respiratory distress
A patient with a large scalp laceration and controlled bleeding
A patient in cardiac arrest without bystander CPR

questions.text: A patient is exhibiting signs of cardiogenic shock following a large myocardial infarction. Which hemodynamic finding would be expected in this patient?
questions.title: Cardiogenic Shock
questions.rationale: Cardiogenic shock is characterized by pump failure leading to decreased cardiac output. This results in a decreased cardiac index (normal range is 2.5-4 L/min/m²). Elevated systemic vascular resistance (SVR) is a compensatory mechanism to maintain blood pressure despite decreased cardiac output. Increased pulmonary artery wedge pressure (PAWP) occurs due to left ventricular failure causing blood backup in the pulmonary circulation.

questions.text: A patient with a diagnosis of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) is starting a new medication regimen. Which combination of medications represents the current guideline-directed medical therapy for this condition?
questions.title: Heart Failure Management
questions.rationale: Current guideline-directed medical therapy for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction includes an ACE inhibitor or ARB (or ARNI like sacubitril/valsartan), a beta-blocker, and a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone). This combination has been shown to reduce mortality and hospitalizations. Digoxin is no longer considered first-line therapy, and calcium channel blockers may be harmful in HFrEF.

choices.text: Lisinopril (ACE inhibitor), metoprolol (beta-blocker), and spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist)
Digoxin, furosemide (loop diuretic), and amlodipine (calcium channel blocker)
Hydralazine (vasodilator), isosorbide dinitrate (nitrate), and diltiazem (calcium channel blocker)
Furosemide (loop diuretic), amlodipine (calcium channel blocker), and atorvastatin (statin)

questions.text: A patient is admitted with hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered mental status, and a core temperature of 105°F (40.6°C) after hiking in hot weather. The most appropriate initial intervention is:
questions.title: Heat-Related Emergencies
questions.rationale: Rapid cooling is the priority intervention for heat stroke, which is a life-threatening emergency. Immersion in ice water or application of ice packs to groin, axillae, and neck along with fans provides the fastest cooling methods. Maintaining airway, breathing, and circulation is always important, but in heat stroke, rapid cooling takes precedence to prevent organ damage. Administration of antipyretics is ineffective because heat stroke is not mediated by pyrogens.

choices.text: Rapid cooling with ice packs to groin, axillae, and neck
Gradual cooling with tepid sponge baths

questions.text: A 72-year-old patient with a history of heart failure presents with new onset of fatigue, palpitations, and mild shortness of breath. The ECG shows an irregularly irregular rhythm without P waves. Which medication would be most appropriate to control the ventricular rate in this patient?
questions.title: Atrial Fibrillation Management
questions.rationale: The patient is showing signs of atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular rhythm without P waves). In a patient with heart failure, metoprolol (a beta-blocker) is appropriate for rate control. Diltiazem and verapamil (calcium channel blockers) are contraindicated in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction as they can worsen cardiac output. Amiodarone is more commonly used for rhythm control rather than initial rate control.

questions.text: During a code blue, a patient is in pulseless ventricular tachycardia. After defibrillation and epinephrine administration, which medication should be considered next according to ACLS protocols?
questions.title: Code Blue Management
questions.rationale: According to ACLS protocols for pulseless ventricular tachycardia, after defibrillation and epinephrine, amiodarone is the antiarrhythmic of choice. The recommended dose is 300 mg IV push for the first dose. Lidocaine is an alternative if amiodarone is unavailable. Atropine is used for symptomatic bradycardia and asystole. Adenosine is used for stable supraventricular tachycardia.

questions.text: A patient with a suspected traumatic brain injury has the following findings: unequal pupils, declining level of consciousness, and increasing systolic blood pressure with widening pulse pressure and bradycardia. These findings suggest:
questions.title: Traumatic Brain Injury
questions.rationale: The classic Cushing's triad of increased systolic blood pressure, bradycardia, and irregular respirations indicates increased intracranial pressure. Along with unequal pupils and declining level of consciousness, these signs indicate the brain is being compressed, likely due to cerebral edema or intracranial bleeding following traumatic brain injury. This is a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate intervention.

questions.text: A 50-year-old patient in the cardiac care unit has a temporary transcutaneous pacemaker with the rate set at 70 beats/minute and the milliamperes (mA) set at 50. The ECG shows pacemaker spikes but no QRS complexes following the spikes. What action should the nurse take first?
questions.title: Pacemaker Management
questions.rationale: Pacemaker spikes without QRS complexes indicate failure to capture. The first intervention should be to increase the milliamperage (mA), which increases the electrical stimulus strength to improve capture. After ensuring proper pad placement, increasing the energy output is the most direct way to address lack of capture. Increasing the rate would not solve the capture problem.

choices.text: Decrease the pacemaker rate to 60 beats/minute
Change the pacemaker to asynchronous mode

questions.text: What is the most important nursing intervention when caring for a patient with a rattlesnake bite to the lower extremity?
questions.title: Envenomation Management
questions.rationale: For venomous snakebites, keeping the affected extremity immobilized and positioned below heart level is critical to slow venom spread. Cold therapy is contraindicated as it can increase tissue damage. Incision and suction are no longer recommended and can worsen outcomes. Elevation would increase venom circulation to the heart and central circulation. Early antivenom administration is also crucial but would be initiated after stabilization and assessment.

choices.text: Apply a tourniquet proximal to the bite
Immobilize the extremity and keep it positioned below heart level
Apply ice to the bite area
Make incisions across the fang marks and apply suction

questions.text: A patient with multiple organ dysfunction syndrome (MODS) following severe sepsis has the following laboratory values: BUN 60 mg/dL, creatinine 3.5 mg/dL, AST 450 U/L, ALT 520 U/L, total bilirubin 4.0 mg/dL, platelets 50,000/mm³. These values indicate dysfunction of which organ systems?
questions.title: Multiple Organ Dysfunction Syndrome
questions.rationale: The laboratory values indicate dysfunction of the kidney (elevated BUN and creatinine), liver (elevated AST, ALT, and bilirubin), and hematologic system (thrombocytopenia with platelets <150,000/mm³). This combination represents multiple organ dysfunction syndrome, which can develop after severe sepsis or other systemic inflammatory conditions.

choices.text: Kidney, cardiovascular system, and neurological system

questions.text: A 60-year-old patient with aortic stenosis reports episodes of syncope with exertion. Which other symptom is commonly associated with severe aortic stenosis?
questions.title: Valvular Heart Disease
questions.rationale: The classic triad of symptoms in severe aortic stenosis includes angina, syncope, and heart failure (dyspnea). These symptoms occur due to fixed cardiac output that cannot increase with demand, leading to myocardial ischemia (angina), cerebral hypoperfusion (syncope), and pulmonary congestion (dyspnea). Palpitations and edema may be present but are less specific for aortic stenosis. Fever would suggest endocarditis, not uncomplicated aortic stenosis.
